name: Study 6: Sacrificing the Rook to Queen

comments.text:
Welcome to another game I played online.

As you can notice, both me and my opponent had a rook and two pieces.

As far as the pawns were concerned, they were up a pawn and had a passed g-pawn.

However, their e-pawns were doubled and weak and I had the majority on the queenside!
I would have captured, of course...
I just activated my king further.
Trying to come closer, but too far away.
A draw.
At this point, it was most logical to trade and simplify the position.
And I continued pushing.
Sliding to b8 now.

But I had Ka8.

Now ...Rh8+ runs into b8=Q.
But my king hid on a7!
But they fell into a fork!
Black brought the bishop back...
But that didn't happen.

Although counterintuitive and very hard to play, going after the pawns is what's correct here.

I can't play ...a7, as capturing the b5-pawn comes with check for Black.

And there wouldn't have been another way of defending my pawns.

They attacked my pawns, though.

There was only one thing I could do here.

This is one of the lines that could arise.

Here, I wouldn't be able to defend my b-pawn.

But that would be fine!
A check on f7 is extremely strong here.
Capturing back.

Black has ...Kf4!

Now the king prevents my rook from sliding to e3.

Black gets a queen here and wins.

It's amazing how much ahead I would have had to think had this line happened!
A draw.

In this position, Black brought the king to d6, escaping from Rf7+.

However, that was a mistake!
A check from the b8-square.

As now I pushed.

In this position, my opponent finally resigned, as I was about to get a queen.

What an interesting game!
I would still bring the king.

I simply got my king to help.

There was no stopping the pawns now!
Whatever I do...
Moving to a1 here wouldn't have been any different.
Another check by Black.
Before capturing, I decided to give another check.
They pinned the b-pawn now.

I went back, attacking the bishop on c2.

My pawn was still sufficiently defended and ready to roll forward.

Important to note.

If Black moves to f2 with the idea of covering the e3-square...
And I had to give up my rook!
One square away, and the knight covers a4.
But I was one square away.
Now I had the luxury of playing b6.

Black had their king to come to the rescue.

The computer still evaluates this position as equal!

Black definitely made the e-pawn become dangerous.
Black can sacrifice the rook.
I captured it, with an attack on the knight.
Black had to capture here...
I have to move my king to e5!
Remove the bishop and keep the only thing that can lead me to victory!

Black captured, which was a blunder!

It was too dangerous to leave me with the two pawns.
This position is equal, since Black would have to give up the rook to stop my passed pawns!

Another check.

The only way to prolong the game.
After Black moves...
Moving the king to the c-file loses.

I played an unnecessary king move here. 

I could have just pushed a7.
I brought the knight back to defend it, though.

Even though Black was a rook up, they were on thin ice here.

My pawns were extremely dangerous and one small mistake could cost them the game!

They gave a check here.
I slid the king up to c3.

I pushed to a6 here, maybe a little bit prematurely.

The computer recommended Rf2+, pushing the king back, and then moving forward with the pawn.
Black can come down with the rook.
I attacked the knight one more time.
I decided to activate my own rook and pressure the bishop on c2.
Black escaped to e3.
The only way to stop the pawn.
Black captured.
I simply captured it.

Black made a mistake here and pinned my knight to the pawn!

But there was no reason for them to get off the b-file.

Black decided to play defense for a moment and bring the rook back.

They probably wanted to place the rook on the e-file and support their pawn.
Going back.
Black would have gotten the queen, yes...
Now I simply pushed the b-pawn and defended my knight.
But I would have taken it.
Now Black could push with tempo on my rook.
Unafraid, I started pushing first!
After the rook moves...

I get the e5-pawn!

Here, the rook and the g-pawn would also have been under attack.
Continuing to push.

Moving up the board.

Black still had the strong and passed e5-pawn, though, which I had to be careful about!

I get the g-pawn as well.

This position would now have been much easier to play!
Black gets to the pawns.
And I recaptured.

They gave another check, that didn't mean anything.

They were already lost by this point.
But I sacrifice it and we arrive to a similar position as the one that happened.
A check.
The king moved over to f4, opening the path for Black's pawn.
But when I give a check...
Wherever Black moves...
They decided to attack my b3-pawn.
They might as well sac the rook.
Black tried moving across the eighth rank...
Black got the knight to d5, giving me another check!
But abandoned the e4-pawn while doing so!
Black would have had ...Rd8+.
Captures.

Black got the rook to g7, attacking my knight.

However, they left the e6-pawn hanging!

Or did they?
Black decided to capture...

And I reactivated my bishop on c4.

Notice how all of my three pieces were attacking Black's weakness on e6.

Putting as much pressure on weaknesses as possible is crucial in endgames.

Which is exactly what happened.

Me and my opponent were both in time trouble, so they played the game out instead of resigning, in hopes that I run out of time.
Black rotated to h5, with ideas of placing the knight on the nice f4-outpost.
Of course, Black captured.
They can attack my rook...

And I finally captured on a6.

Although I was up a pawn with two connected passed pawns on the queenside, the game still wasn't clear.

Black had a rook on the third rank and a passed pawn of their own!
And Black proceeded with their plan, putting pressure on my bishop.

I couldn't stop ...e2 now, so I activated my king.

And prepared for what was about to arrive.
I would have had this check...
Although my king was now cut off, I decided to go after the bishop on f5.
Black has the same idea...
Instead of recapturing and letting me queen, Black has ...Ra4, stopping the pawn.
I didn't take advantage of it immediately and played Rf6, targeting the g- and the e-pawn.
Whatever I do...
I can protect the queening square from e3.
Black pushed...

I can defend.

But there is no way for Black to win here anyway.

I played the most logical move here.

Kept the rook on the second rank, disallowing ...e2, while supporting my own a-pawn.
Black had no choice but to try and create counterplay by pushing themselves.
It didn't matter, though.

I continued with c4, trying to trade off a pawn on the queenside.

That's where I had more pawns, after all.

I can pin the pawn with Ra2.

Now Black's bishop is hanging!
Black defended the e-pawn, but there wasn't much they could do about the g-pawn.
We could maybe say that these checks only helped me move my king up the board.
